reviews.review_text:
It is a canonical work of rarely seen proportions.

[i]Baldur’s Gate[/i] is the role-playing game that set the standard for fantasy RPGs for decades, and if one were to compare its status and importance to works within cinema, it could fairly be called the [i]Citizen Kane[/i] of role-playing games. That is how significant and influential [i]BioWare[/i]’s 1998 classic truly is.

From a gameplay perspective, it feels close to perfection, even if younger players may initially find parts of it dated. However, if they give the game a genuine chance, I am convinced they will discover its greatness — especially thanks to the excellent “Enhanced Edition” releases by [i]Overhaul Games[/i]. The controls remain intuitive, and with the enormous flexibility surrounding difficulty options, the game is perfectly suited for both hardcore veterans and complete newcomers.

The game’s mise-en-scène is genuinely genre-defining, filled with varied and memorable environments. Personal highlights include the forests surrounding the Wood of Sharp Teeth. The colours of the trees still strike me during every playthrough as absolutely stunning. The world feels cohesive and alive, and the way characters react to your actions creates an impressive sense of immersion — especially for those who experienced the game in the late 1990s.

The dialogue is also of the highest quality, with a sense of humour that was never fully recaptured in [i]Siege of Dragonspear[/i], even if I still appreciate that expansion greatly, and perhaps above all for its ambition. The tone balances the deep darkness at the heart of the narrative with the adventurous spirit that defines most of the player’s journey. The pacing suits me perfectly as well, with a mystery that slowly grows larger and more ominous the longer you play, before culminating in a grand finale.

I have completed this game at least fifty times, and I will continue returning to this legendary adventure — especially in its refined Enhanced Edition form. In truth, I can barely mention a single negative aspect, simply because I am far too much of a fan, even if I fully understand why certain elements may frustrate some players. I loved the game when it released shortly before the turn of the millennium, and somehow, I love it even more today.

If you have the time, and are willing to invest yourself in a truly extraordinary role-playing game, then this is the ultimate place to begin.

ok so, ill get right to the point on this one:

if you are coming here looking for a nostalgic old dungeons and dragons game with an engaging story and fun characters, you have come to the right place!

HOWEVER, if you are coming here just after playing baldurs gate 3 then chances are that i would NOT recommend this game. this game is to old and clunky by today's standards and doesn't even make sense sometimes (ie: saving throws).

considering all this i guess its a little bit silly that i played and 100% this game because i actually DON'T like old d&d.
I bought this game because i wanted to click on monsters to make my level go up and it lets you do that so it's good

Baldur’s Gate is a foundational CRPG that still delivers a deep and rewarding adventure. This is old school D&D through and through, with it's combat style, party management, and a sprawling world that rewards patience and exploration. The sense of journey is fantastic - starting as a low-level nobody and slowly building into something powerful feels incredibly satisfying. The writing and characters add a lot of charm, even if they’re a bit simpler compared to modern RPGs.

That said, it definitely shows its age. The interface can be clunky, combat can feel chaotic until you understand it, and there’s a learning curve that might turn off newcomers. But once it clicks, it’s hard to put down. It captures that tabletop D&D spirit better than most games, and it’s easy to see why it became such an influential classic. Not the easiest game to get into today, but absolutely worth the effort if you do.

It's so peak, so much flavor in the game. Something about the difficulty in getting into the systems makes the immersion better. The challenge is just right to be rewarding and you disappear into the world. Love it

I have played this game twice now, once from disks may years ago form CDs and the second time was this steam playthrough. I enjoy playing the game and very much like all the different side quests and random (sometimes very random) things characters are doing in the game.

Its genuinely a game you play and feel like you are making the game your own in the choices you make. I only really choose the good side of things and always play a sorcerer half elf and found the game challenging especially during the harder fights and end boss fights [spoiler] (I had to use potions (loads of potions for everyone in the party and I don't mean healing potions) to win the final battle of Dragon Spear - BG1.5) [/spoiler].

My real motivation for playing this game again was to play BG1 then BG2 and finally BG3 obviously this play though overran a bit (since its now 2026 - I pre-purchased BG3 and have yet to play it!).

I think its a really good game but I much preferred BG2 (and its expansion) in terms of actually playing the game. To compare the two I have now started my third play through of BG2 (on steam), which is an extra play through to BG1.

Anyway I would certainly recommend this game for anyone who likes these kind of games or for those who want to take part in and know the plot before (and take a single character though BG1, BG2 and presumably BG3 - no idea if you can import into BG3) playing BG3.
Old school D&D party adventures.  Nice mix of party interactions and local quests with some great FAQs to help when you get lost.

Played this in the late 90s and never finished due to being a pot head teenager.  Wish I hadn't waited so long to come back to it.  
I am a big fan of the Pillars of Eternity & Pathfinder games on PC.  There is so much the BGEE does that feels awkward at first, but just slow down and get your bearings.  

The starting area has tutors that I felt explained things pretty damn well.  Tons and tons of videos and forums explaining things differently as well.  

THAC0 is not scary.  I wish YouTubers and reviewers wouldn't trash it so much, it's just a system where the math is a little different.  It's still basic arithmetic.  Modern games like WH40K: Rogue Trader are way more complicated and people are doing fine there too.  If anyone tells you it's awful, they're just being dramatic.

The story is pretty straight forward.  The only timed quests are companion quests, but they're super easy and they'll warn you if you're taking too long.  

Difficulty - If you raise the difficulty, I recommend turning off increased damage with higher difficulty.  It's a fantastic option to keep it both challenging and fun.

This is a game where you should not feel bad reloading if a companion dies.  It's going to happen a lot, and resurrection fees can be rough in the early game.  

Anyway, I started on Core difficulty for my first playthrough.  I started over and raised it to Insanity and still had a blast.  I normally don't do that, especially on my first full run.  

The game also has story mode to prevent you from dying.  Totally recommend that as well.  There's something to be said about just kicking back and exploring all the maps.  They have so many little stories tucked away with cool rewards.

Very basic story, it's sort of a prototype for Bioware (rest in peace) and what they'd come out with later. Don't expect a lot of dialogue from your companions, or an incredibly engaging main story. This game is largely about the D&D combat and little else. Not quite on the level of say, Icewind Dale, but it's not nearly as story intensive as every Bioware game that comes after it. Especially Baldurs Gate 2.

The story can essentially be summed up as "some guy killed your dad, are you a bad enough dude to kill him back?" The answer is of course yes.

Fun! Really fun. Great storytelling, no 3d to speak of so runs perfectly even on potato PC with no dedicated graphics. Doesn't even try to use my dedicated card.

Some things are RATHER difficult to get used to at first. The interface is overwhelming and confusing at first. The rules probably aren't the same as the DnD you've played lately because this game, as far as I can tell, uses mostly 2e rules, instead of more recent editions like 5e that BG3 used. And, more than anything, the combat encounters being "real time with pause" is disorienting at first. It took me a couple times pushing myself to try the game before it really clicked but I'm glad it did, because it's totally worth it. Butt kicking for goodness!

one of my all time favorites! love that they did an EE for modern systems. however....... whom ever at beamdog wrote pure garbage like neera as a npc yah. just fire them terrible addons. stick to the game itself and it pure bliss skip the EE beamdog addons.
Great Story, amazing soundtrack, still holding up the combat.
Great game...old, but great still. Highly recommended. It's based on AD&D Edition 2, but easy to pick-up if you're used to another Edition, or, indeed, no AD&D at all. :)

After completing BG3 for the 8th time I did revisit BG1 and I must say it still holds up as a great rpg. Yes, it is dated, the graphics and game play make it very challenging compared to more modern rpgs but its a really good story with great NPCs. 

I have completed BG1 3 times, each time with a slightly different party. It's great and a classic. I do miss a party of 6 characters in later games. I do believe a 6 character group should the be default.

On the Mass Effect Scale, Baldur's Gate gets willing participation in 5 out of 6 genocides. Thoughts on game sectioned and labelled below.

It's Old Caveat:
Baldur's Gate is older than me with a release date of 1998 which comes with old gaming systems. It is not a game I would recommend for a quick adventure, it comes with commitment to a game designed to be played on a screen smaller than a phone, with 2nd edition Dnd rules, and a point and click rpg, which all have their caveats. The textures are grainy and warped, but for a game so old they translate wonderfully to modern equipment, and the only real problem is that some models and textures that the designers choose to use are just ugly. 2nd edition DnD is something that most people have never touched, but the basic premises of the system are the same that every single person who has touched a video game should understand. Plenty of Beamdog post and other online forums speak as if 2nd edition DnD takes years of training to understand but a couple of hours looking at the basic rules of the edition are more than enough jump into the game. Having said that, the game is still unforgiving in "incorrect" point allocations and by the end of the game if the player has choosen a bad class, or allocated points in the wrong direction it will be difficult to defeat the final boss. This happened to me in both BG1 and BG2, where at the final boss my player character was completely useless due to me specking fully into summoning... The only way I beat Baldurs Gate 1 is through lucky rolls, and BG2 I only beat because the final bosses character AI broke after i reloaded for the 50th time. So unless you really want to play this game a few times, were a single playthrough can be 20 hours, the game should be played on the normal difficulties, like most other old rpg games. And finally it is a point and click adventure. Like other point and click adventures it is slow and it is sometimes difficult to see. In comparison to my only frame of reference the first Fallout games, Baldur's Gate is much easier to operate and see in. Unlike the fallout series there is highlight interactables button, which makes searching through an area trivial.

Exploration:
Blew me away in this game. I don't think a single game has ever used a completely blank map like Baldur's Gate 1 to such a good effect as BG1 does. Your party is given a limited field of vision, and to see anything in a loading area the objects in screen have to fall within your party's field of vision to be revealed. Every step you take can lead you into a wyvern or a cliff-face, you can be storming to the top of a hobgoblin camp to rescue the witch that has enchanted the most noble of warriors and his [spoiler]giant space hamster[/spoiler] or going to the back exit of the camp taking the wrong stair case. In a normal game these results might seem like bad design, but because of how well crafted Baldur's Gate is this exploration is fun. If the player tries to interact with people in town (plays the game) and doesn't randomly wander off into the wilderness, the npcs and lore will tell the player what to expect on their explorations. This makes every mission in the wild a treasure hunt, were you are given all the information you need to find the location but along the way you'll bump into fun dialogue, a suprise attack, or a new side-quest the player can follow. Being in the dark sounds inherently unfun, but due to the effort that the Bioware puts in, its actually an incredible mechanic, which I have not seen used to the same effect anywhere else. The one problem with this mechanic is its setting specificity. It really only works in the wilderness environment that Baldurs Gate mostly takes place in. At the end of the game, when you enter Baldur's Gate finally (oddly you don't enter this city again until Baldur's Gate III) the mechanic stops working. I'm not entirely sure why this is the case, but maybe its due to the locations in a city being suprisingly less distinct, where it becomes difficult to distinguish between one elaborately designed house and another, especially when your field of vision won't reveal the entire model. Bioware recognized this problem and in the sequel all cities are fully revealed upon entrance, but even with this caveat this sole mechanic makes me want to play the game a second time.

Story:
The only problem with the story is that it doesn't achieve the same perfection as the sequel story. Taken by itself it is still very good. However, it is not exactly what a modern player would expect. It is written like a DnD campaign because it was a DnD campaign. Bioware literally just published a DnD campaign that the office played together and rather liked. That's exactly the story the game was goes for though. BG1 is in the end a DnD game, so for the story to be a direct rip from a DnD campaign only makes sense. This results in a story that has an overarching plot that feels like a backwater to whatever the player main quest is right now. Do I really care about the main bad guy while I'm diving into the [spoiler] silver mine's to fight lunatic kobolds[/spoiler]? Not really. But this is the story the game is trying to tell, one were the plot cares more about what the player is doing right now than fighting the villain. Its fun and it works.

Combat:
Its servicable. You select your characters, queue up attacks, and react to the attacks that the enemy is doing. Most of the attacks that the enemy uses aren't really reactable, like if a Dragon decides to turn you to stone, your not going to be able to tell your character to cast the anti-stone turning spell, however (once you turn on the dialogue box so you know what moves the enemy is casts) after the fight you have two options. Either you reload the game to cast protection spells on your fighters before the battle to prevent the petrifying gaze spell or you have been delivered a quest to learn the spell depetrify to free your companion turned to stone. I for the most part went with option 1, because usually its not turned to stone, but something more like instant death for your whole party. What this results in is the supposed problem of DnD before edition 5 (i think, I'm no expert), doing party wide casting buffs before every single fight. In Baldur's Gate 3 there is something called concentration which limits the amount of buffs that a party can cast on each other. This is not the case in BG or BG2, so instead the player is encouraged to cast all their combat spells before every fight in the game, giving their party like +5 stats to everything and preventing some of the nastier spells. It's pretty boring and I am glad that they removed this in the 5th edition, but the cycle of figuring out what sort of damage an enemy is weak to, what spells they use, and how much damage they do is fun and most fights in the game don't need every spell to be cast.

Side Notes:
I wrote this review a year or so after I finished this game, but the entire BG series are such good games that they will be enternally locked in my memory, even though the story of this game isn't as stellar as every single Bioware game, its still one of the best games I've ever played.
I can see why this game is considered revolutionary, it is very fun especially if you like dnd but dont have friends to play with
Great story, playability, customization, treasure-hunting

Well worth on it's own though.

However.. be prepared to save scum A LOT. I'm only playing on the normal difficulty and i have to reset every so often bcus of character quest time limits or bcus every chapter has certain quests which you CANNOT go back to.
